item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i can't go one day without seeing a candy corn elf webcomic reference, can't i?

jesus it's like dude i dont wanna hear no homestuck im sorry :C

item.title: Re: ~Necropost~
item.content:encoded: You've got the option of being a bit more explicit about the effect due to the therapy sessions. I don't know that you'd want to hit people over the head with it, but some mention of Li's cognitive dissonance stemming from a conflict between her deep desire to reconnect and her actual recollections of (perhaps) a troubling family relationship would do the trick.

item.title: ~Necropost~
item.content:encoded:
I would definitely recommend focusing more on Li and how the "urge to return home/speak with family members" symptom isn't actually what it first appears to be. As is, we only see the fact that Li talks about wanting to go home but declines to actually do so, and the fact that the son referenced in Document 2397-1 never actually met or became aware of his father. Those are decent hints, but they're pretty subtle, and weakened by being in conflict with the description of how the area makes you want to go home, which is established early on and therefore has primacy in the reader's mind.

There are ideas that are very effective when very little is said about them (to let the reader develop their own theories, or to let them grapple with the uncertainty of what the missing information is), and this site has a reputation for that sort of thing- but I think your idealized-nostalgic-homesickness concept would be better conveyed with more than the current level of description.

If the description of Junior Researcher Li's reactions and of Mr. S's history are strong enough, you might be able to drop Document 2397-1 entirely (which I gather would be an improvement in some readers' eyes). If the events documented in the article give us a good enough impression of what the effect is and where it might have come from, we can draw our own conclusions about the intent behind it, and the SCP doesn't need to explain itself in its own words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original intent was that Researcher Li would, by some external circumstance (maybe the Foundation made her take a vacation?) be forced to return home for a period, and would find herself upset and bewildered, thus solidifying the already alluded-to separation between the ideal home that subjects (and the original resident of the area) long for vs. the much more flawed home that they originally left. I wasn't sure how to make that work, though, and I feared it would make the article even cheesier than it already is, so I held back. I'm not sure now, though. Do you think it'd be better with that in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish the conclusion was built on more. This feels unfinished, which is frustrating because what's here is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if the animals know they're in Iowa. We should check to see what kind of identification cards the animals have on them to test this theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. It reminds me of the Wyoming problem, but not in a "this is like another article" way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If that's your intention, my advice (take it or leave it) is to remove the note and find a way to communicate the idealization aspect, perhaps through Junior Researcher Li, without outright saying it (which is easier said than done, of course). Alternatively, take out the note and simply allow the rest to remain a bit vague. Like Mulciber, I too found the core idea to be compelling. The weirdness of part of Alaska being Iowa can be enough for me. Don't underestimate the power of weirdness and mystery, especially when the writer knows the answer to the mystery and can let the story flow organically from it.

By the way, your choice of states on its own communicates quite a lot. Iowa evokes the idea of the American heartland, while Alaska is known as a place where a person can go to escape a life they want to leave behind. Well done in that regard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Article backstory: this thing was conceived and written within about a six-hour window. I showed it to several staff, who said it was okay, so the version I posted was actually my first draft. The original idea was that it makes you miss an idealized version of home that takes away your ability to confront the real, actual home, but that sorta got lost because I wasn't sure how to convey it. It's fairly likely that I'll revise this thing at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you explain what you enjoyed about the original concept? I don't feel like there's anything especially compelling about a part of Alaska that is in fact Iowa. If there's a better angle for me to be going at with this, though, I'd love to hear from you, since I'm not really happy with this thing myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the initial concept of an anomalous patch of Iowa appearing in Alaska, but the explanation was unsatisfying and bland. This was a good start concluded by an ineffective tug-on-the-heartstrings ghost story I didn't find compelling at all, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this interesting, being an Alaskan who has made trips to Iowa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2397 is a square region of land, 1.3 kilometers to a side, located several miles outside of the small town of █████, Alaska, near the side of US Route ███.

Alaska does not have any US Routes, just state highways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that the the 'note from the SCP at the end, trying as hard as it can to force feels' thing is rather overdone by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's not much wrong with this. You have the story, you have the grammar, and you have the well done procedures. You've read the guides. My only problem is that it's just not grabbing me. I'm just not intrigued by the object itself.

Due to the well done execution, but the lack of grabbing me, I'll cancel out to a no-vote.
